skill.name: caveman-review
skill.description: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
skill.summary: Ultra-compressed code review comments for faster, clearer PR feedback.
skill.author: juliusbrussee

security_audit.summary: All 13 static analysis findings are false positives. The static scanner misidentified markdown backtick inline code formatting as Ruby/shell command execution across 11 locations. The network and weak-crypto detections at lines 3 and 7 are also incorrect — those lines contain YAML frontmatter metadata and trigger descriptions, not actual network calls or cryptographic operations. This skill is a pure markdown instruction file that defines code review formatting behavior for AI agents. It contains no executable code, no network requests, no command execution, and no file system operations. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Review code with compressed one-line comments
content.value_statement: Code review feedback is often verbose and repetitive, burying actionable items in polite filler. This skill formats each review finding as a single line with exact location, problem, and fix.
actual_capabilities: Formats each code review finding as one line: location, problem, and suggested fix
Uses severity prefixes (bug, risk, nit, question) to prioritize feedback
Drops to normal prose for security findings and architectural disagreements that need full explanation
Includes exact line numbers, symbol names, and concrete fix suggestions
Persists review mode until the user disables it or ends the session
limitations: Output only — does not write code fixes, approve changes, or run linters
Requires the user to provide code or a diff to review; cannot fetch remote pull requests
Compressed format may be unsuitable for junior developers who need more context
Auto-clarity overrides apply to security findings and architectural disagreements only

use_cases.title: Fast PR review for busy teams
use_cases.description: Review pull requests with concise one-line comments that highlight bugs, risks, and suggestions without verbose explanations.
use_cases.target_user: Senior developers reviewing many pull requests daily

use_cases.title: Standardized review comments across teams
use_cases.description: Apply consistent formatting to all code review feedback so every comment follows the same location-problem-fix pattern.

use_cases.title: Self-review before submitting a pull request
use_cases.description: Ask the AI to review your own code diff before opening a pull request, catching issues early with minimal noise.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Simple review request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code and tell me what to fix.
prompt_templates.scenario: You just finished a feature and want a quick check before opening a pull request.

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Review with severity levels
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this diff and use severity prefixes for each finding.
prompt_templates.scenario: You are reviewing a teammate's pull request and want clear, prioritized feedback.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Multi-file diff review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this multi-file diff. Include file names with line references for each finding.
prompt_templates.scenario: A large pull request touches five files. You need organized, file-scoped comments.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Security-focused review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for security vulnerabilities. Use full explanations for any security findings.
prompt_templates.scenario: A security-sensitive module needs review. The auto-clarity rule will expand security findings into full paragraphs.

output_examples.input: Review a function that fetches user data without error handling
output: L12: 🔴 bug: fetchUser returns null on miss. Add null check before .email access.
L15: 🟡 risk: no timeout on fetch call. Add AbortController with 5s deadline.
L8-22: 🔵 nit: 15-line fn could extract error handler. Reduce duplication.

output_examples.input: Review a multi-file pull request adding a new API endpoint
output: routes/user.js:L34: 🔴 bug: req.params.id passed directly to query. Use parameterized query.
routes/user.js:L41: 🟡 risk: no rate limit on POST /users. Add express-rate-limit middleware.
tests/user.test.js:L5: ❓ q: test covers happy path only. Should 400 and 500 cases be added?

best_practices: Provide the code or diff directly in your prompt so the review has concrete material to analyze
Use severity prefixes to communicate urgency at a glance: bug for broken code, risk for fragile patterns, nit for style suggestions
Request full explanations for security findings by relying on the built-in auto-clarity rule that expands those automatically
anti_patterns: Asking the skill to write the actual code fix — it only outputs review comments, not patches
Expecting approval or change-request actions — the skill produces comments ready to paste into a pull request but does not interact with the platform
Using compressed format for onboarding new developers who need more context and rationale for each suggestion

faq.question: What format does each review comment use?
faq.answer: Each comment is one line: L followed by the line number, a severity prefix, the problem description, and a concrete fix suggestion.

faq.question: How do I activate caveman-review mode?
faq.answer: Say "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", use "/review", or invoke /caveman-review. The mode persists until you disable it.

faq.question: Can this skill write the actual code fix for me?
faq.answer: No. Caveman-review only outputs review comments. It does not write code fixes, approve changes, or run linters.

faq.question: What do the severity prefixes mean?
faq.answer: bug means broken behavior that will cause an incident. risk means the code works but is fragile. nit means a style or naming suggestion that can be ignored. q means a genuine question, not a suggestion.

faq.question: Will security findings still get detailed explanations?
faq.answer: Yes. The auto-clarity rule automatically drops terse mode for security findings and provides full explanations with references.

faq.question: How do I turn off caveman-review mode?
faq.answer: Say "stop caveman-review" or "normal mode" to revert to standard verbose review style.
